node.text:
Consumer perception appears far from historical reality. While the June inflation report has dominated headlines this week, it is important for investors to maintain a longer-run mindset. The outlook may seem bleak, but the data shows that investors tend to be overly pessimistic about the future of stocks. The New York Federal Reserve conducts an ongoing survey of U.S. consumers that asks if they expect stock prices to be higher a year from now. On average, over the past eight years, only four out of every 10 respondents have thought so. However, the reality—based on historical S&P 500 returns starting from 1950—is that the likelihood of achieving a positive return one year later is 80%, roughly double that of consumer perception. Blind optimism can hurt portfolio performance in the short term, but allowing pessimism to derail your long-term plan can be even more detrimental to reaching your goals.

For more on how investors can invest during volatility, tap the link in our bio to explore our #MidYearOutlook.

node.text:
The interest-rate-sensitive housing market may be a harbinger for the broader economy. Mortgage rates have surged higher amid the Fed’s aggressive tightening campaign to fight inflation. Between that and the appreciation of home values, the monthly cost of owning a house has surged over the past year. As a result, housing activity like home sales has moved sharply lower. That’s the Fed’s goal: Slow activity down, so that inflation follows.
 
For more on where we stand with inflation, tap the link in our bio to check out last week’s #TopMarketTakeaways.
 
Chart 1 Source: Redfin, Bankrate.com, Haver Analytics, Bloomberg Finance L.P. Data as of April 30, 2022. *Monthly mortgage payment assumes a 20% down payment on price of median U.S. single family home and the national average 30-year fixed mortgage rate at the time specified.
 
Chart 2 Source: National Association of Realtors, Bloomberg Finance L.P. Data as of April 30, 2022.

node.text:
Sentiment remains poor. Uncertainty emanating from elevated inflation, continued lockdowns in Asia and a global commodity supply crunch are plaguing the economic landscape. As such, it’s perhaps unsurprising that the UMichigan Consumer Sentiment Survey is at the lowest level since 2011. That said, history suggests that periods marked by this much pessimism may actually present compelling market entry points for long-term investors. Looking back at the last four times the index reached levels comparable to today, the S&P 500 returned an average of +20% over the next 12 months.
 
For more on recent developments and how they affect the team’s investment decisions, tap the link in our bio to read last week’s #TopMarketTakeaways.
 
Source: Bloomberg Finance L.P. Data as of May 17, 2022.

node.text:
Volatility continues to be the word of 2022. This year marks the fourth-worst start of the year* for the S&P 500 on record, with a maximum drawdown of -17.8%, as persistent inflation and the Federal Reserve’s response are causing investors’ concern. But with volatility comes potential opportunity, and this market is presenting some of the most compelling entry points in several years for long-term investors.

To find out where we see opportunity, tap the link in our bio to read our latest insights in #TopMarketTakeaways.

Source: Bloomberg Finance L.P. Data as of May 18, 2022. *Worst start of the year is defined as the largest drawdown from January 1 to May 18 of each respective year.

node.text:
The Federal Reserve is continuing its journey to slow growth and inflation. Last week, they announced a 50bps interest rate hike—double the size of the first 25bps hike delivered in March. Some market watchers have pointed to the 1994–1995 hiking cycle as an analog, which consisted of seven interest rate hikes in just over a year (Chart 1). A key difference between then and now is that inflation is materially higher today than in 1994 (Chart 2). The Punchline: The likelihood the Fed tames inflation without unnerving the equity markets looks difficult, but possible (Chart 3).
 
For more insights on the journey of the Fed and the recent market volatility, tap the link our bio to check out last week’s #TopMarketTakeaways.
 
Sources: [1] Bloomberg Finance L.P. Data as of May 11, 2022 [2] Bureau of Economic Statistics, Haver Analytics. Data as of April 30, 2022. [3] Bloomberg Finance L.P. Data as of May 11, 2022. Note: Data for both hiking cycles begins one month prior to first hike.  
 
*Past performance is no guarantee of future results. You cannot invest directly in an index.

node.text:
How high can yields go? As the Federal Reserve signals more rate hikes are coming (and fast), bond yields around the globe have moved higher. While equity markets have moved lower as a result, investors can now earn a yield in fixed income not seen in several years. We think it’s time to once again focus on core bonds which can provide defensiveness, diversification and an added bonus of yield generation in portfolios.
 
For more on our latest insights and the bull case to be made in today’s environment, be sure to read last week’s #TopMarketTakeaways.
 
Source: Bloomberg Finance L.P. Data as of April 22, 2022. *Note: TEY calculated assuming a 40.8% tax rate.

*Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

node.text:
Headline inflation came in at 8.5% year-over-year in March, validating the Fed’s urgency to combat it with aggressive rate hikes. Over 80% of items in the Consumer Price Index (CPI) report saw their prices increase by over 4% year-over-year, the highest share since 1981. This share is up from a mere 5% in 2019 and 70% in January of this year. Digging deeper, only 2% of items in the CPI saw a decline in prices last month, including smartphones and TVs. From here, we expect overall inflation to decelerate… it’s just a matter of when and at what pace.
 
For more insights on how to prepare portfolios for late cycle, be sure to check out last week’s #TopMarketTakeaways.
 
Source: Bureau of Labor Statistics, Haver Analytics. Data as of March 2022

*Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

node.text:
Even as fears of a rapidly maturing economic cycle emerge, we’re still seeing strong gains in the labor market. The March data released last week marked the 11th month that the economy has added more than 400,000 jobs—a record streak. Higher interest rates, higher prices and downtrodden sentiment could mean that jobs growth slows from here, but the data isn’t pointing in that direction yet. All else equal, more jobs support more spending across the economy—suggesting that the economic expansion will persist for the foreseeable future.
 
For more on what to expect as we continue through the business cycle, tap the link in our bio to read last week’s #TopMarketTakeaways.
 
Sources: BLS, Haver Analytics. Data as of March 2022.

*Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

node.text:
The first quarter of this year has felt rough for the stock market, but believe it or not, it’s been worse for bonds. Indeed, given the sharp move higher in interest rates this year, the investment-grade bond index is down almost 7% year-to-date, underperforming the S&P 500 by almost 2.5%. In fact, this has been the worst start to a year for investment-grade bonds in at least the last 30 years. Investors could start to look for opportunities in core fixed income amidst the sell-off.
 
For more investment insights, tap the link in our bio to check out last week’s #TopMarketTakeaways.
 
Source: Bloomberg Finance L.P. Data as of March 25, 2022. Note: U.S. stocks and bonds represented by the S&P 500 and Bloomberg U.S. Agg respectively.

*Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

node.text:
AI is shaking things up faster than most people expected, especially in the tech world. Last week, software company stocks took a big hit after new AI tools showed they could handle tasks like organizing data, tracking expenses and creating reports—things that used to require expensive software programs. Investors are worried that these traditional software companies may become obsolete.

Whether or not these concerns become reality, companies already using AI are seeing real benefits. Their margin expansion has outpaced that of companies that haven't yet adopted AI by over 3 percentage points. Disruption can have painful consequences, but the revenue opportunity and productivity gains will be worth it.

Tap the link in bio to know more.

As proud sponsors of the Sundance Film Festival, we were thrilled to bring together 200+ clients and guests for our final “Cocktails & Conversations” event in Park City, Utah. We heard an inspiring conversation about storytelling and the future of the film industry with one of Hollywood’s most versatile and dynamic talents, Olivia Wilde. 

The award-winning actor, director, producer and co-founder of investment firm Proximity Ventures recently celebrated her Sundance directorial debut with her latest film, “The Invite.” Thank you to Olivia and to all attendees for a truly memorable evening. 

To learn more about entertainment tap the link in bio.

node.text:
The past few weeks have been wild for gold. After surging to nearly $5,600/oz, gold experienced its steepest daily drop since 2013 on Friday at -8.9%, and its largest intraday decline in over a century at -14.0%. Despite this volatility, we believe gold will continue to benefit from investors and central banks seeking to marginally diversify how they store their wealth.

Zooming out, the yellow metal has long been the ultimate safe haven—a store of value that has endured for thousands of years. When markets feel uneasy, even with concerns about the U.S. dollar, investors often turn to gold. Historically, periods marked by such uncertainty in the U.S. dollar have coincided with surges in gold prices, reinforcing gold’s role as an effective diversifier.

node.text:
Unveiling the 2026 Global Family Office Report, a comprehensive look at how family offices worldwide are navigating new investment approaches and generational priorities. With insights from 333 family offices across 30 countries, discover how families are embracing AI, refining succession plans, and safeguarding portfolios against inflation and geopolitical risks. Explore the key findings and see how families are building resilience for the future. 

Tap the link in bio to download the full report.

#FamilyOfficeReport

node.text:
Performance isn’t just for athletes. As this episode’s guest David Howden proves, it is also essential in business. The energetic founder of the global insurance group Howden credits resilience and teamwork for his extraordinary success, believing that mistakes should be embraced as a sign of growth—not failure.

Our partnership with Performance People brings you candid insights from top leaders in sports and business. 

Tap the link in bio to watch the full podcast now.

node.text:
Founders spend years, decades and sometimes entire lifetimes creating companies. When the time comes to sell, we support them as they navigate the complexities of that transition, helping to shape a legacy that endures long after the transaction is complete.

“From evolution to exit” is our all-inclusive resource hub that empowers business leaders to shape their liquidity events with clarity and confidence. Within the campaign, our U.K. spotlight series draws on the perspectives of Private Bank leaders as they explore defining moments in this journey.

Join Marice Brown, UK Region Head, and James Chilvers, Head of UK Wealth Advisory, for a conversation on how the firm can best assist entrepreneurs in their ventures.

To discover more insights, tap the link in the bio.

node.text:
Chess takes center stage on the J.P. Morgan NextList 2026—a curated collection of books and experiences to spark big ideas and exploration. The legendary musical returns to the Imperial Theatre after 37 years, featuring ABBA’s iconic music and starring Aaron Tveit, Lea Michele and Nicholas Christopher. On opening night, JPM’s Darin Oduyoye joined the cast and guests on the red carpet to share why this bold revival is captivating audiences today.

Tap the link in bio to learn more.

node.text: We were proud to host our 18th Annual Women in Healthcare event in San Francisco in honor of the J.P. Morgan Healthcare Conference. Attendees gathered at the St. Regis hotel for a reception celebrating women executives and investment professionals, and heard from JPMorganChase leaders Jamie Dimon, Chairman and CEO, and Dorothee Blessing, Global Co-Head of Investment Banking Coverage, who shared their perspectives on the year ahead.

Tap the link in bio to learn more.

In this year's Eye on the Market Outlook: Smothering Heights, Michael Cembalest, Chairman of Market and Investment Strategy, highlights that most direct AI companies are showing strong free cash flow, with hyperscalers investing 35%-40% of revenue in capex and R&D, and Meta at nearly 70%. These firms are betting big on AI.

Tap the link in the bio to read the full Outlook.
